questions.prompt: قلبو من دهب means:
options: He has a heart of gold / He's incredibly kind
questions.explanation: قلبو من دهب (albo min dahab) literally means 'his heart is of gold' — a Lebanese expression praising someone's kindness, not their wealth. دهب = gold refers to character, not riches.

questions.prompt: الصبر مفتاح الفرج means:
options: Patience is the key to relief
questions.explanation: This is a classic Arabic proverb: الصبر (patience) + مفتاح (key) + الفرج (relief/ease). It teaches that patience leads to resolution — a deeply valued virtue in Lebanese culture.

questions.prompt: نوّرتو البيت is said when:
questions.explanation: نوّرتو البيت (nawwarto l-bēt) literally means 'you lit up the house' — a warm Lebanese hospitality phrase used to welcome guests. It's figurative, not about actual lights.

questions.prompt: In the doctor dialogue, ضغط means:
questions.explanation: ضغط (daght) means pressure — used for both blood pressure (ضغط الدم) and stress in Lebanese Arabic. حرارة = fever. دوا = medicine.

questions.prompt: What does the conditional لو كنت بعرف mean?
options: If I had known (but I didn't)
questions.explanation: لو (law) + كنت (I was) + بعرف (I know) forms the unreal conditional — 'if I had known (but I didn't)'. لو implies the condition was NOT met, unlike إذا which is neutral. This is essential for expressing regret in Lebanese.

questions.prompt: What does قالتلي إنها مريضة mean?
options: She told me that she is sick
She asked me if I'm sick
They said we are all sick
questions.explanation: قالتلي (āletli) = she told me, إنها (innha) = that she, مريضة (mrīḍa) = sick. This is reported speech in Lebanese Arabic. The suffix -لي on the verb adds 'to me', and إنّ introduces the reported clause.

questions.prompt: The slang expression يي means:
questions.explanation: يي (yii) is a Lebanese exclamation expressing surprise, dismay, or shock — similar to 'oh!' or 'wow!'. It's very colloquial and used constantly in daily speech. Sometimes drawn out as يييي for emphasis.

questions.prompt: ما كنت حكيتلو لو ما سألني means:
questions.promptArabic: ما كنت حكيتلو لو ما سألني
options: I will tell him if he asks me
I wouldn't have told him if he hadn't asked me
I always tell him when he asks
He told me not to ask
questions.explanation: This is a past unreal conditional: ما كنت حكيتلو (I wouldn't have told him) + لو ما سألني (if he hadn't asked me). The ما...لو ما pattern creates 'wouldn't have...if not' — a key advanced structure.

questions.prompt: The discourse marker هلق in conversation means:
questions.explanation: هلق (halla') primarily means 'now' but in conversation it often functions as a discourse marker meaning 'so' or 'well' when shifting topics. Example: هلق شو بدنا نعمل؟ = So, what do we do now? It's one of the most characteristically Lebanese words.

questions.prompt: What does the idiom عينو وسيعة mean?
questions.explanation: عينو وسيعة (ʕayno wēsʕa) literally means 'his eye is wide' — a Lebanese idiom meaning someone is greedy or never satisfied. The opposite عينو ضيقة (narrow eye) means stingy. Lebanese body-part idioms are very common.

questions.prompt: How do you say "supposedly" or "apparently" in Lebanese slang?
questions.explanation: شكلو (shaklo) literally means 'its appearance' but is used colloquially for 'apparently' or 'it seems like'. Example: شكلو رح تشتي = it looks like it's going to rain. يعني = like/meaning, هيك = like this, مبين = visible.

questions.prompt: بلا ما يحكي كلمة means:
questions.explanation: بلا ما (bala ma) = without, يحكي (yiḥki) = he speaks, كلمة (kilme) = a word. So بلا ما يحكي كلمة = without saying a word. بلا ما is the Lebanese way to say 'without' before a verb clause.

title: Phase 5 Fill in the Blank — Advanced Patterns

fillBlanks.sentence: ___ كنت بعرف، كنت ساعدتك means 'If I had known, I would have helped you'.
fillBlanks.hint: The unreal conditional particle — implies the condition was NOT met.

fillBlanks.sentence: قالّي ___ مش جاي بكرا means 'He told me that he is not coming tomorrow'.
fillBlanks.hint: The Lebanese complementizer meaning 'that' in reported speech.

fillBlanks.sentence: The idiom ___ من دهب means "heart of gold".
fillBlanks.hint: The word for 'his heart' in Lebanese.

fillBlanks.sentence: The slang word ___ means "it seems / apparently".
fillBlanks.hint: Literally means 'its appearance' — very common in casual speech.

fillBlanks.sentence: ___ شو بدنا نعمل هلق؟ means 'So, what are we going to do now?'
fillBlanks.hint: A discourse marker meaning 'ok/so/well' to introduce a new topic.

fillBlanks.sentence: عينو ___ means 'he's greedy' (literally 'his eye is wide').
fillBlanks.hint: The adjective meaning 'wide' — opposite of ضيقة (narrow).

fillBlanks.sentence: ما كنت ___ لو ما حكيتلي means 'I wouldn't have known if you hadn't told me'.
fillBlanks.hint: Past tense of عرف (to know) for 'I'.

fillBlanks.sentence: The expression يا ___ is said when something bad happens, meaning "What a pity!"
fillBlanks.hint: Also used to express sympathy — 'poor thing!'

matchingPairs.english: He has a heart of gold (very kind)
He steals / is a thief (lit. 'long hand')
He talks too much / is rude (lit. 'long tongue')
Looks like it's going to rain
It doesn't matter to me / I don't mind

title: Phase 5 Quiz v2 — Media, Slang & Debate

questions.prompt: In a news broadcast, نشرة الأخبار means:
questions.explanation: نشرة الأخبار (nishrit il-akhbār) is the standard term for a news bulletin or newscast. نشرة means bulletin/broadcast and الأخبار means the news. You'll hear this at the start of every Lebanese TV news program.

questions.prompt: The youth slang expression أكيد أكيد is used to mean:
questions.explanation: أكيد (akīd) means 'sure/certain'. Doubling it — أكيد أكيد — is a common Lebanese intensifier meaning 'absolutely, 100%'. Youth often use repetition for emphasis in colloquial speech.

questions.prompt: In an academic context, أطروحة refers to:
questions.explanation: أطروحة (uṭrūḥa) means thesis or dissertation. It comes from the root ط-ر-ح (to put forward/propose). Lebanese university students use this when discussing their academic research. رسالة is also used for a master's thesis.

questions.prompt: During a debate, بالعكس is used to say:
options: On the contrary / quite the opposite
questions.explanation: بالعكس (bil-ʕaks) means 'on the contrary' — a key debate expression in Lebanese Arabic. It's used to politely introduce an opposing viewpoint. العكس means 'the opposite', so بالعكس literally = 'by the opposite'.

questions.prompt: The expression مقهور/ة conveys a feeling of:
questions.explanation: مقهور (ma'hūr, m.) / مقهورة (ma'hūra, f.) describes a deep emotional state of feeling oppressed, frustrated, or wronged by an unjust situation. It's stronger than زعلان (upset) and carries a sense of helplessness. Very common in Lebanese emotional expression.

questions.prompt: The slang term فلّ means:
questions.explanation: فلّ (fall) is Lebanese youth slang meaning 'he left' or 'he bounced'. It's very informal. Example: فلّ من الحفلة بكّير = He bounced from the party early. The formal equivalent is راح or مشي.

questions.prompt: مراسل صحفي refers to:
questions.explanation: مراسل صحفي (murāsil ṣaḥafi) = news correspondent/reporter. مراسل comes from the root ر-س-ل (to send) — literally 'one who is sent'. صحفي means journalistic (from صحافة = journalism). This is standard media vocabulary across Arabic dialects.

questions.prompt: In a heated discussion, خلّيني كمّل is used to say:
questions.explanation: خلّيني كمّل (khallīni kammil) = 'let me finish'. خلّيني = let me / allow me, كمّل = finish/continue. This is an essential debate and conversation phrase when someone is being interrupted. Very commonly heard in Lebanese discussions and talk shows.

sentenceBuilderData.english: Did you see what was on the news today?
On the contrary, this is the best decision.
If I had studied more, I would have passed.
Let me finish the idea, then speak.
She told me that she's frustrated by the situation.
